results.title: Agentcard: debit cards for AI agents.
results.content:
# the team

Karen (CEO): former CEO @ Atlas (acquired by Remote), then turned down a job at Stripe to build Agentcard instead.

Felipe (CTO): second-time YC founder (HelloGuru, W22) and first employee at Rappi (YC W16), where he shipped consumer products at scale.

# the problem

agents can now research, plan, and decide but the second they need to pay, they're stuck. the workarounds are all bad: hand your agent your real card and you've got no limits and no controls (one bad prompt from disaster), or build your own issuing + checkout stack and lose months becoming a payments company. so agents do the first 90% and hand checkout back to you, the most valuable step is the one they can't take.

# the solution

Agentcard is everything an agent needs to pay for things online: [...] all of it works with any agent: over MCP, the CLI, or our API. and people are already using it: agents out there paying for OpenAI subscriptions, groceries and infra on their own.

# our ask

 building with agents? try it → agentcard.sh, and tell us which merchant we should add next
 building a product whose agents should buy things? let's talk → founders@agentcard.sh

thanks everyone 🙏

Agentcard

Agentcard

Batch:Summer 2026

Status:
Active

Location:San Francisco

Primary Partner:Tyler Bosmeny [...] See original launch post

hey everyone, we're Karen and Felipe, and we are building Agentcard: debit cards for AI agents.

# TL;DR

AI agents are finally smart enough to do real things for you. but the second they need to pay for something, they're stuck, and handing an agent your real card (no limits, no controls) is a terrible idea.

so we built Agentcard: give your agent its own debit card: fund a wallet, set a budget, and issue one-time cards that auto-close after a single charge. works with any agent over MCP, the CLI, or our API. its everything an agent needs to pay for things online. here's the lineup:

so we built Agentcard — everything an agent needs to pay for things online. here's the lineup

# the team

results.title: AgentCard: A Framework-Neutral Identity and Capability ...
results.content:
4. Physically grounded resource accounting. AI computation has non-zero energy cost. AgentCard includes an optional pricing field whose minimum value is constrained by Landauer's principle [LANDAUER], preventing fraudulent "zero-cost" capability claims in multi-agent economic systems.¶
5. Extensibility without breakage. New fields MAY be added to future versions of AgentCard. Readers MUST ignore unknown fields. The metadata object provides a namespaced extension point for framework- and application- specific annotations.¶ [...] ## Abstract

This document defines AgentCard, a lightweight JSON data format that enables autonomous software agents to declare their identity, capabilities, communication endpoints, and resource pricing in a framework-neutral manner. AgentCard is designed for agent-to-agent (A2A) communication scenarios where agents built with different frameworks — such as LangChain, CrewAI, AutoGen, or custom implementations — must interoperate without prior coordination.¶

An AgentCard is a pure data schema: it carries no execution logic and imposes no transport requirements. It is analogous to an HTTP response header set — a machine-parseable self-description that any compliant reader can interpret.¶ [...] ## 4. Well-Known URI Discovery

An agent that serves its AgentCard at a well-known HTTP(S) endpoint SHOULD publish it at the path `/.well-known/agentcard` relative to the agent's base URL (see Section 9.2).¶

For example, an agent at ` SHOULD publish its AgentCard at:¶

```
GET  Accept: application/agentcard+json HTTP/1.1 200 OK Content-Type: application/agentcard+json Cache-Control: max-age=3600 { "agent_id": "01HZQK3P8EMXR9V7T5N2W4J6C0", "name": "ExampleAgent", ... } 
```

¶

The response SHOULD include a `Cache-Control` header. AgentCards are relatively stable documents; a max-age of 3600 seconds (one hour) is RECOMMENDED. Validators and registries SHOULD re-fetch at the cache expiry interval.¶

results.title: FAQ — Common Questions About AgentCard Virtual Cards | AgentCard
results.content:
### What if my agent goes rogue and overspends?

It can't. AgentCard uses virtual cards with fixed balances — your agent can only spend what's loaded on the card. Load $25 and that's the hard limit. You can also freeze or revoke the card instantly from the CLI.

### How do I set spending limits on my AI agent?

Every AgentCard is a virtual card with a fixed balance. The balance IS the spending limit — your agent physically cannot exceed it. For additional control, you can create separate cards for different tasks or agents, each with its own budget.

### Is my card data secure?

Yes. AgentCard is built by Alchemy. Card details (PAN, CVV) are encrypted at rest with AES-256-GCM. Authentication uses HTTPS-only session tokens. We never sell your data to third parties. [...] ### Can I use AgentCard with Claude Code or Cursor?

Yes. AgentCard works natively with Claude Code, Cursor, and any MCP-compatible client. Install the CLI, buy a card, and your agent can retrieve card details directly. AgentCard also exposes a skill endpoint at agentcard.ai/skill for AI-native integrations.

### Where are AgentCard cards accepted?

AgentCard cards are accepted at supported online merchants that accept card payments, including services like Vercel, GitHub, Figma, AWS, Namecheap, Amazon, and DoorDash.

## Security & Spending Limits

### What if my agent goes rogue and overspends? [...] ### Can I get a refund on unused card balance?

Yes. Refunds are fully supported — any remaining balance on a card you've purchased can be fully refunded back to your original payment method.

### Can I top up an existing card?

Currently, each card is purchased with a fixed balance. To add more funds, buy a new card. We're working on top-up support.

## Still have questions?

Check out thequickstart docs or get started in under 60 seconds.

```
npm install -g agentcard
```
